title: Full exam simulations
summary: Two complete past-exam simulations worked end to end. Each exam is three multi-part problems — planar mechanism kinematics (ICR + rigid-body relations), single-DoF free vibrations of a pivoted bar, and motion transmission (gear trains, helical tooth forces, belt-and-band-brake braking). A short theory refresher gives the three reliable solving recipes, then all six sub-problems are solved with full method, formulas, final answers and traps.
objectives: Recognize the three standard exam problem types (kinematics, vibrations, transmission) and the method each one demands
Locate the instantaneous centre of rotation and apply the rigid-body velocity and acceleration equations, including the centripetal term and guide constraints
Reduce a pivoted bar with springs and a damper to a single-DoF canonic equation and extract ω_n, ζ and ω_d
Compute gear-train ratios, reflected inertias and torques using power balance with η = 1
Resolve spur and helical tooth forces into tangential, radial and axial components using the transverse plane quantities
Analyse a belt-plus-band-brake braking system: ratio, capstan tensions, reflected rope tension, deceleration and stop time

blocks.text: How the Applied Mechanics exam is built

The written exam is **three multi-part worked problems**, always in the same flavours:

- **Problem 1 — planar kinematics of a mechanism** (slider-crank, four-bar, rollers in guides). You are given one point's velocity and acceleration and must find the velocity/acceleration of another point plus the angular velocity/acceleration of a link, usually via the **instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR)** and the **rigid-body velocity/acceleration equations**.
- **Problem 2 — dynamics / free vibrations** of a pivoted bar with springs and a damper. You build the **single-DoF equation of motion**, reduce it to canonic form and read off $\omega_n$, $\zeta$, $\omega_d$.
- **Problem 3 — motion transmission**: gear trains (spur or helical), drums, belts, brakes. You compute transmission ratios, torques and exchanged forces using **power balance** ($\eta=1$ here) and tooth-force geometry.

The professor uses the easier first parts to check you understand the **method**. Always: (1) draw the mechanism, (2) write the constraint each body obeys, (3) apply the rigid-body relations or a power/torque balance, (4) carry signs and senses, (5) state units.

The instantaneous centre $C$ of a link is the point with zero velocity. It lies at the intersection of the lines drawn **perpendicular to the velocity of two points** of the link. Then $\omega=\dfrac{v_P}{\overline{CP}}$ for every point $P$, and $v_P\perp\overline{CP}$. If the two perpendiculars are parallel, $C$ is at infinity and the link is in **pure translation** ($\omega=0$).

blocks.tex: \vec v_B=\vec v_A+\vec\omega\times\vec r_{AB},\qquad \vec a_B=\vec a_A+\vec\alpha\times\vec r_{AB}-\omega^2\,\vec r_{AB}

blocks.text: These two relations solve every kinematics problem. Pick the point whose motion you know fully ($A$), the point you want ($B$), and impose the **constraint** on $B$ (e.g. "$B$ stays on a horizontal guide" $\Rightarrow v_{By}=0,\,a_{By}=0$; "$B$ on a circle of radius $r$" $\Rightarrow$ centripetal $v^2/r$ toward the centre).

For a pivoted bar, reduce springs/dampers to the rotation $\vartheta$ about the pivot: a spring at distance $d$ contributes $k\,d^2$ to $k_{eq}$, a damper at distance $d$ contributes $c\,d^2$. The bar's inertia about the pivot $O$ (end-pivot) is $I_O=\tfrac{1}{3}mL^2$ (from $I_G=\tfrac{1}{12}mL^2$ + parallel axis).

Spur gears: pitch radius $r=\tfrac{m\,z}{2}$, ratio $i=\tfrac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}}=\prod\tfrac{z_{driven}}{z_{driver}}$. Power conserved with $\eta=1$: $P=C\,\omega$ constant $\Rightarrow C_{out}=i\,C_{in}$. Tangential tooth force $F_t=\tfrac{C}{r}$; radial $F_r=F_t\tan\alpha$; normal $F_n=\tfrac{F_t}{\cos\alpha}$. For helical gears use the **transverse** quantities: $m_t=\tfrac{m_n}{\cos\beta}$, $\tan\alpha_t=\tfrac{\tan\alpha_n}{\cos\beta}$, and axial force $F_a=F_t\tan\beta$.

steps.label: Sketch & constraints
steps.text: Redraw the mechanism, mark known $\vec v$, $\vec a$, and write what each joint forces ($v\perp$ link for a pin on a rotating bar; $v$ along the guide for a slider; $v^2/r$ centripetal on a circular guide).

steps.label: Velocities
steps.text: Find the ICR (or use $\vec v_B=\vec v_A+\vec\omega\times\vec r_{AB}$). Get $\omega$ and the unknown velocity. State the sense (CW/CCW, left/right).

steps.label: Accelerations
steps.text: Apply $\vec a_B=\vec a_A+\vec\alpha\times\vec r_{AB}-\omega^2\vec r_{AB}$ with the constraint on $B$. Solve the two scalar equations for $\alpha$ and the unknown acceleration component.

steps.label: Dynamics/transmission
steps.text: For vibrations: torque balance about the pivot, reduce to canonic form. For transmission: ratio from teeth/radii, then power/torque balance ($\eta=1$) and tooth-force geometry.

steps.label: Check
steps.text: Units, order of magnitude, and that any brake/resistance produces a **deceleration** (resisting torque larger than driving).

blocks.text: Classic mistakes: (1) forgetting the **centripetal $-\omega^2 r_{AB}$** term in the acceleration equation; (2) using $I_G=\tfrac{1}{12}mL^2$ instead of $I_O=\tfrac{1}{3}mL^2$ for an end-pivoted bar; (3) for helical gears, plugging the **normal** module/pressure angle into the centre-distance instead of the **transverse** ones; (4) dropping the sense (CW/CCW) — the professor grades it.

question.prompt: At an instant the velocities of two points of a rigid link are both horizontal. Where is the link's instantaneous centre of rotation?

options.content: At infinity — the link is in pure translation, so $\omega=0$
At the faster of the two points
Undefined; the link cannot be rigid

question.explanation: The ICR is where the perpendiculars to the two velocities meet. Two horizontal velocities give two parallel (vertical) perpendiculars, which meet only at infinity: the link instantaneously translates and $\omega=0$ (though $\alpha$ may be non-zero).

question.prompt: A uniform bar of mass $m$, length $L$ is pivoted at one end $O$ and carries a spring $k$ at its tip. The small-oscillation natural frequency is:
question.explanation: Reduce to rotation about $O$: $k_{eq}=kL^2$ (spring at distance $L$) and $I_O=\tfrac13 mL^2$ (end-pivoted bar, parallel-axis from $\tfrac1{12}mL^2$). Then $\omega_n=\sqrt{k_{eq}/I_O}=\sqrt{3k/m}$.
